threats.title: rentalWallet Omitted from _deriveRentalOrderHash Enables Order Hash Collision and State Corruption
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveRentalOrderHash function excludes the rentalWallet field from the EIP-712 encoding despite the type string declaring 'address rentalWallet'. This means RentalOrder structs differing only in rentalWallet produce identical hashes. An attacker calling stopRent can substitute a different rentalWallet in the order struct and still pass the hash existence check in Storage.removeRentals. This causes assets to be reclaimed from the wrong Safe, rentalAsset counters for the wrong wallet to be decremented, and the original wallet's rental counters to remain permanently elevated, locking assets behind the guard forever.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect: the abi.encode call in _deriveRentalOrderHash goes from order.renter directly to order.startTimestamp, skipping order.rentalWallet. The hash output is identical regardless of rentalWallet value, allowing callers to supply arbitrary wallet addresses to stopRent while matching stored order hashes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode skips order.rentalWallet: encodes order.renter then order.startTimestamp directly, missing the rentalWallet field between them

preconditions: An active rental order exists in Storage.orders
Attacker knows all fields of the active rental order (emitted in RentalOrderStarted event)
A substitute rental safe (protocol-deployed) holds matching ERC721/ERC1155 tokens for the reclaim to succeed, OR for ERC1155 the rentedAssets count at the substitute wallet must not underflow

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: abi.encode(_RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH, order.seaportOrderHash, ..., order.renter, order.startTimestamp, order.endTimestamp) — rentalWallet absent
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Caller of stopRent provides fabricated rentalWallet that matches a different Safe, while the computed hash still equals the stored order hash
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can force reclaim from wrong safe (stealing ERC721/ERC1155 if present), permanently lock original safe's assets (rentedAssets never decremented), and potentially corrupt escrow settlement

economic_questions: Can an attacker reliably create conditions where a substitute safe holds matching rental tokens?
What is the economic impact of permanently locking assets in the original safe?
Can ERC1155 amounts > 1 create scenarios where the substitute safe's rentedAssets don't underflow?
validation_requirements: Construct two rental safes with same NFT types, verify stopRent succeeds with swapped rentalWallet
Verify rentedAssets for original safe remain elevated after exploit
Check if escrow payment settlement sends funds to correct lender/renter despite wallet swap

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of creating a rental + acquiring matching NFTs for substitute safe
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of stolen NFTs from substitute safe + permanent lockup of original safe assets

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Targeted theft, no market manipulation needed

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Owner of substitute safe loses NFTs; original rental participants have assets permanently locked
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full loss of rented NFT value + locked payments
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All active rentals with matching ERC1155 tokens across safes

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare hash output with and without rentalWallet modification
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify the abi.encode field ordering against EIP-712 type string

threats.title: orderType and emittedExtraData Omitted from _deriveOrderMetadataHash Allowing Fulfiller to Alter Order Semantics
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveOrderMetadataHash function only encodes rentDuration and hooks into the hash, omitting orderType and emittedExtraData despite both being declared in the OrderMetadata EIP-712 type string. This means the lender's Seaport zone hash does not cryptographically bind the order type. A fulfiller can substitute orderType without invalidating the zone hash check in _isValidOrderMetadata. While current item-type validation in _convertToItems mitigates cross-type conversion for most cases, the missing binding violates the security model and any future code path that relaxes item constraints would enable payment flow reversal.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code clearly shows only two fields encoded: _ORDER_METADATA_TYPEHASH, metadata.rentDuration, keccak256(abi.encodePacked(hookHashes)). The type string declares 4 fields: orderType, rentDuration, hooks, emittedExtraData. This is a demonstrable encoding mismatch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode(_ORDER_METADATA_TYPEHASH, metadata.rentDuration, keccak256(hookHashes)) — metadata.orderType and keccak256(metadata.emittedExtraData) are absent

preconditions: Fulfiller receives a valid server-side signature and payload for an order
Fulfiller modifies metadata.orderType before calling Seaport fulfill

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Only 2 of 4 declared fields encoded in metadata hash
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Fulfiller changes orderType from BASE to PAY or vice versa without invalidating zoneHash
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Currently mitigated by item-type validation but emittedExtraData injection is immediately exploitable for event log manipulation; orderType swap becomes exploitable with any future relaxation of item constraints

economic_questions: Are there any edge cases in _convertToItems where a BASE order's item structure passes PAY validation?
Can emittedExtraData manipulation cause downstream issues for off-chain indexers?
What economic impact does event data injection have on protocol integrations?
validation_requirements: Fuzz all combinations of orderType substitution against _convertToItems validation
Verify emittedExtraData can be set to arbitrary values without validation
Test if any order structure passes validation under multiple orderType values

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Event log manipulation immediate; full payment flow reversal if validation gaps found
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable - depends on exploitable edge cases

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Lender whose order type intent is violated
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Potential full payment reversal if exploitable
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rental payment values if type switching succeeds

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map all valid item configurations per orderType
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm metadata hash only includes 2 of 4 type-string fields

threats.title: Guard Completely Bypassed When Hook Is Active — Rented Assets Transferable
threats.hypothesis_description: In Guard.checkTransaction, when a hook is registered and active for a target contract address, execution flow is forwarded entirely to the hook via _forwardToHook and the _checkTransaction function is NOT called. This means all standard protections (blocking NFT transfers, approvals, module changes, guard removal) are completely delegated to the hook contract. If a hook has any bug, missing check, or doesn't replicate all guard protections, rented assets can be transferred out of the safe. The guard provides ZERO fallback enforcement when a hook is active.
threats.investigation_rationale: The if/else branch at lines where hook != address(0) && isActive clearly shows _forwardToHook is called and _checkTransaction is in the else branch, meaning they are mutually exclusive. A single buggy hook compromises all guard protections for that target.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (hook != address(0) && isActive) { _forwardToHook(...); } else { _checkTransaction(...); } — mutually exclusive branches, hook bypasses all standard checks

preconditions: GUARD_ADMIN has set a hook for a target contract address
The hook contract does not replicate all Guard._checkTransaction protections
Renter initiates a transaction from the rental safe to the hooked target

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Hook receives onTransaction call but no minimum enforcement requirements exist in the protocol
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Renter calls transfer/approve on hooked target; hook doesn't block it; _checkTransaction never runs
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full theft of rented NFTs if hook doesn't independently implement rental asset protection

economic_questions: What is the expected interface contract for hooks? Do they receive enough data to enforce rental checks?
Are there deployed hooks and do they replicate guard protections?
Can a renter target a hooked contract that is also a rented NFT's contract?
validation_requirements: Deploy a hook that doesn't revert, verify rented NFT can be transferred via hooked target
Test calling setGuard or enableModule on hooked targets
Verify hooks receive sufficient state to enforce rental checks

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of renting an NFT whose contract has an active hook

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When a hook is set for the rented NFT's contract

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Lender who provided the NFT for rental
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All NFTs rented through hooked contract targets

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Enumerate all deployed hooks and their protection implementations
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm _checkTransaction is unreachable when hook is active

threats.title: Guard Bypass via Non-Standard NFT Function Selectors Not Covered by _checkTransaction
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard._checkTransaction only blocks a hardcoded set of ERC721/ERC1155 function selectors (transferFrom, safeTransferFrom, approve, setApprovalForAll, safeBatchTransferFrom). Any non-standard transfer or approval function (e.g., EIP-4494 permit, custom burn, permit-and-transfer patterns) is NOT blocked. If a rented NFT implements additional transfer mechanisms beyond the standard selectors, the renter can use these to move or approve rented assets out of the safe. The guard's else branch for unknown selectors does nothing except check the three specific known-bad selectors.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _checkTransaction function uses an if/else if chain for specific selectors. Any selector not in this chain falls through to the final else block which only checks three hardcoded selectors. All other selectors are implicitly ALLOWED.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Hardcoded selector checks only cover 5 ERC721 and 2 ERC1155 selectors plus 3 Safe selectors. All other selectors pass through without blocking.

preconditions: Rented NFT implements a transfer or approval mechanism with a function selector not in the guard's hardcoded list
Active rental exists with such an NFT in the safe
No hook is active for the NFT contract (or hook doesn't block the selector either)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Guard only checks ~10 selectors; ERC721/ERC1155 contracts can implement arbitrary additional functions
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Renter calls permit(spender, tokenId, deadline, sig) or custom transferNFT() on the rented asset; guard does not block
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full theft of rented NFT via non-standard transfer/approval mechanism

economic_questions: Which popular NFT collections implement EIP-4494 permit or other non-standard transfer mechanisms?
What percentage of rentable NFTs have non-standard transfer functions?
Can burn() functions be used to destroy rented assets?
validation_requirements: Deploy an NFT with EIP-4494 permit, rent it, call permit from safe, verify guard allows it
Enumerate common non-standard NFT selectors and test against guard
Check if approve-by-permit-then-external-transfer works

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of fulfilling a rental order for a target NFT
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of the rented NFT

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Lender of NFTs with non-standard transfer mechanisms
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rentals involving NFTs with non-standard selectors

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Catalog all non-standard selectors in top NFT collections
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm unmatched selectors pass through guard without blocking

threats.title: Whitelisted Delegate Call Target Can Bypass Guard and Steal All Rented Assets via Batched Internal Calls
threats.hypothesis_description: If a contract like Gnosis MultiSend is whitelisted as a delegate call target (via toggleWhitelistDelegate), a rental safe owner can delegatecall to it and execute arbitrary internal calls that bypass the guard entirely. When a safe delegates to MultiSend, the internal calls execute in the safe's context but do NOT pass through the safe's execTransaction flow, meaning the guard's checkTransaction is never invoked for those internal calls. The renter can batch transferFrom calls for all rented NFTs within a single MultiSend delegatecall.
threats.investigation_rationale: Guard.checkTransaction allows delegatecalls to whitelisted targets. Delegatecall to a batching contract like MultiSend allows arbitrary internal calls that bypass the guard. Additionally, checkAfterExecution is empty, providing no post-execution verification that assets are still in the safe or that guard/module configuration is preserved.
deployment_assumptions: A batching/multicall contract (e.g., MultiSend) is whitelisted via toggleWhitelistDelegate
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script DeployProtocol.s.sol does not show specific whitelist entries but the toggleWhitelistDelegate function exists and is callable by ADMIN_ADMIN role.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (operation == Enum.Operation.DelegateCall && !STORE.whitelistedDelegates(to)) revert — whitelisted delegates pass without further inspection of delegated logic

preconditions: A batching/multicall contract is whitelisted as a delegate call target
Renter has an active rental with NFTs in their safe
Renter constructs a MultiSend payload containing NFT transfer calls

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Guard only validates the outer delegatecall target, not internal call payloads
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: DelegateCall to MultiSend → internal transferFrom(safe, attacker, tokenId) calls bypass guard
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All rented NFTs in the safe can be extracted in a single transaction

economic_questions: Is MultiSend or any similar contract currently whitelisted?
Can a whitelisted delegate also overwrite the guard storage slot to permanently disable the guard?
What is the total value of rented assets that could be stolen?
validation_requirements: Whitelist MultiSend, create rental, delegatecall MultiSend with transfer payload
Verify guard is not consulted for internal MultiSend calls
Test if delegatecall can overwrite guard storage slot

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of all rented NFTs in the safe

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders with assets in attacker's rental safe
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rented assets in safes where delegate whitelist is populated

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Query whitelistedDelegates for known batching contracts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm delegatecall internal calls bypass guard flow

threats.title: Hook Status Change After Rental Creation Permanently Locks Rented Assets and Escrowed Payments
threats.hypothesis_description: When stopRent is called, _removeHooks checks if each hook has onStop enabled via STORE.hookOnStop(target). If a GUARD_ADMIN disables a hook's onStop bit (e.g., setting bitmap from 7 to 3) after a rental was created with that hook, stopRent permanently reverts. The lender's NFTs are locked in the renter's safe and escrowed ERC20 payments cannot be settled. There is no alternative mechanism to stop the rental or bypass the hook check.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _removeHooks function reverts on disabled hooks: if (!STORE.hookOnStop(target)) revert. Hook status is mutable via updateHookStatus. No validation exists at hook status change time to check for active rentals using that hook.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (!STORE.hookOnStop(target)) { revert Errors.Shared_DisabledHook(target); } — reverts if hook onStop bit disabled post-creation

preconditions: A rental was created with hooks specified in the order metadata
GUARD_ADMIN changes the hook status bitmap to disable the onStop bit after rental creation
Anyone tries to call stopRent for the affected order

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: hookStatus is mutable but stopRent assumes hooks remain enabled for the rental's lifetime
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: GUARD_ADMIN calls updateHookStatus(hook, newBitmap) where newBitmap doesn't include bit 4 (onStop)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All escrowed payments for affected rentals are permanently locked; all rented NFTs are permanently stuck

economic_questions: How many active rentals use hooks at any given time?
What is the total value at risk across all hook-dependent rentals?
Is there any emergency recovery mechanism?
validation_requirements: Create rental with hook, disable hook onStop bit, attempt stopRent, verify permanent revert
Verify no alternative code path exists to stop the rental
Check if kernel migration could help recover stuck assets

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A (DoS), total locked value depends on active hook-dependent rentals

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Permanent fund lockup for affected rentals

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lenders and renters with active hook-dependent rentals
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of escrowed payment + locked NFT value

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map hook usage across active rentals

threats.title: CEI Violation in stopRent: Reentrancy via NFT Callback Before Storage Removal Enables Cross-Order Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: In stopRent, _reclaimRentedItems transfers NFTs back to the lender via safeTransferFrom, which triggers onERC721Received/onERC1155Received callbacks on the lender. At this point, the order is still active in Storage (orders[hash] == true) and rentalAssets haven't been decremented. A malicious lender contract can re-enter stopRent or stopRentBatch for a DIFFERENT order during this callback, potentially draining escrow funds or corrupting rental asset tracking. In stopRentBatch, the reentrancy window is even wider since all reclaims happen before any settlements or storage removals.
threats.investigation_rationale: The execution order in stopRent is: _removeHooks → _reclaimRentedItems → ESCRW.settlePayment → STORE.removeRentals. External calls with callbacks occur at steps 1 and 2, before any state cleanup at steps 3 and 4. This violates CEI pattern.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _reclaimRentedItems (external NFT transfer with callbacks) at line 226 before STORE.removeRentals at line 237; order still in storage during callback

preconditions: Lender is a smart contract implementing onERC721Received/onERC1155Received
Lender has multiple active rental orders
Lender's callback function re-enters stopRent for a different order

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: External callback occurs while orders[hash] == true and rentedAssets unchanged
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Lender receives NFT callback → re-enters stopRent for order B → order B fully settled and removed → original stopRent continues with potentially altered escrow state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Double-settlement of same tokens if escrow state is corrupted, or DoS of batch operations

economic_questions: Can re-entering stopRent during a callback cause the original stopRent to fail, leaving state inconsistent?
Can the escrow balance underflow if two orders for the same token are settled in unexpected order?
Does stopRentBatch's wider reentrancy window enable more severe exploits?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious lender that re-enters on NFT receipt, test with two orders
Verify escrow balance accounting under reentrancy
Test stopRentBatch reentrancy during first order's reclaim callback

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of creating two rental orders as lender
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Potential double payment extraction or permanent state corruption

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When lender has multiple active orders

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol escrow (fund extraction) or renters (incorrect settlements)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to full order payment value

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map exact state during callback execution
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace escrow balance changes under reentrancy scenarios

threats.title: Empty checkAfterExecution Allows Delegatecall to Overwrite Guard Storage Slot Permanently Disabling Asset Protection
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard.checkAfterExecution is completely empty (no-op). This means after any safe transaction executes—including whitelisted delegatecalls—there is no verification that the safe's guard and module configuration remain intact. A whitelisted delegatecall target could write to the Gnosis Safe's guard storage slot (keccak256('guard_manager.guard.address')) and set it to address(0), permanently disabling the guard. All subsequent transactions from the safe would bypass all rental protections, allowing free transfer of rented assets.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checkAfterExecution function body is empty. Post-execution state is never validated. The Gnosis Safe guard storage slot is at a well-known location and is writable via delegatecall.
deployment_assumptions: A whitelisted delegate call target exists that can write to arbitrary storage slots
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script does not show specific whitelist entries. Guard.checkAfterExecution at Guard.sol has empty implementation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function checkAfterExecution(bytes32 txHash, bool success) external override {} — completely empty, no post-execution validation

preconditions: A whitelisted delegate call target exists
The delegate target has capability to write to arbitrary storage slots (e.g., a generic proxy or storage manipulation contract)
Renter executes delegatecall from safe to overwrite guard slot

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No validation of safe state after any transaction execution
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Delegatecall writes guard slot to address(0) → all future transactions bypass guard → free NFT transfers
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All rented NFTs in affected safe can be stolen after guard removal

economic_questions: What contracts are whitelisted for delegatecall? Can any of them write to arbitrary storage?
What is the Gnosis Safe guard storage slot address?
Could checkAfterExecution detect guard removal by comparing before/after?
validation_requirements: Deploy a delegate target that overwrites guard slot, whitelist it, execute from safe
Verify guard is removed after delegatecall
Verify subsequent NFT transfers succeed without guard

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of all rented NFTs in the safe

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Lender of NFTs in affected safe
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rented assets in safes with populated delegate whitelists

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Gnosis Safe guard storage slot location and current whitelist state

threats.title: PAYEE Orders Have Zero On-Chain Footprint — Assets Unrecoverable by Protocol
threats.hypothesis_description: In Create._rentFromZone, when the order type is PAYEE, the entire storage recording block is skipped. No order hash is stored in Storage.orders, no rental asset updates are recorded, no ERC20 deposit increments occur, and no hooks are processed. PAYEE orders exist only as Seaport order events. If the paired PAY order fails, is never created, or is stopped independently, the PAYEE-side assets (NFTs and ERC20s transferred via Seaport considerations) have no protocol-level tracking and cannot be stopped/reclaimed through the protocol's mechanisms.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _rentFromZone function has an explicit condition: if (payload.metadata.orderType.isBaseOrder() || payload.metadata.orderType.isPayOrder()). PAYEE orders fall into neither branch, so all storage, escrow, and hook logic is bypassed. The validateOrder function still returns success, validating the Seaport order.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (payload.metadata.orderType.isBaseOrder() || payload.metadata.orderType.isPayOrder()) { ... } — PAYEE orders skip entire block containing addRentals, increaseDeposit, and _addHooks

preconditions: A PAYEE order is fulfilled via Seaport
The paired PAY order doesn't exist, fails, or is stopped independently

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: PAYEE orders produce zero storage entries: no orders[hash], no rentedAssets, no balanceOf increment
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: PAYEE-transferred NFTs have no guard protection (not tracked as rented) and can be freely transferred by recipient
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Assets delivered via PAYEE fulfillment are effectively unrecoverable if PAY counterpart fails

economic_questions: Are PAYEE orders always atomic with their PAY counterpart in Seaport batch fulfillment?
Can a PAYEE order be fulfilled independently of its PAY counterpart?
What happens to PAYEE-side ERC20 payments if they're not tracked in escrow?
validation_requirements: Fulfill a PAYEE order independently and verify no storage entries exist
Attempt stopRent on a PAYEE order and verify failure
Check Seaport atomicity guarantees for PAY/PAYEE pairs

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable based on PAYEE order values

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: PAYEE order participants whose assets become untracked
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All PAYEE order values when PAY counterpart fails

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Seaport batch atomicity for PAY/PAYEE pairs
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm PAYEE orders produce zero storage footprint

threats.title: EIP-712 _deriveHookHash Does Not Hash Dynamic bytes Field Per Specification
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveHookHash function passes hook.extraData directly to abi.encode instead of keccak256(hook.extraData) as required by EIP-712 for dynamic 'bytes' types. This means the hook hash computed on-chain differs from what any standard EIP-712 library would produce. If the protocol signer's off-chain system uses the correct EIP-712 encoding (hashing bytes with keccak256), signatures will never verify on-chain, breaking rental creation. If both sides use the wrong encoding, the non-standard encoding could enable hash collision attacks with different extraData values.
threats.investigation_rationale: EIP-712 specification states: 'The dynamic values bytes and string are encoded as a keccak256 hash of their contents.' The code encodes hook.extraData as raw bytes via abi.encode, which includes offset, length, and data — not its keccak256 hash.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode(_HOOK_TYPEHASH, hook.target, hook.itemIndex, hook.extraData) — hook.extraData should be keccak256(hook.extraData) per EIP-712

preconditions: Protocol uses hooks with extraData in rental orders
Off-chain signer or verification tools follow standard EIP-712

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: abi.encode produces offset+length+data encoding for bytes, not keccak256 hash
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Signature mismatch between off-chain (correct) and on-chain (incorrect) encoding, or extraData collision under non-standard encoding
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Integration breakage or potential hash collision enabling unauthorized hook parameters

economic_questions: Does the protocol signer's off-chain system use standard or matching encoding?
Can different extraData values produce the same hook hash under abi.encode?
What economic impact does integration failure have?
validation_requirements: Compute hook hash using both standard EIP-712 and on-chain encoding, compare outputs
Test if protocol signer can produce valid signatures with hooks
Analyze abi.encode collision space for bytes fields

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: DoS of hook-based rentals or unauthorized hook parameters

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Unable to create or stop hook-based rentals

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare on-chain vs off-chain hash computation for hooks with extraData

threats.title: Immutable Domain Separator Enables Cross-Chain Signature Replay After Chain Fork
threats.hypothesis_description: The EIP-712 domain separator is computed at deployment as an immutable value using the deployment-time block.chainid. While _CHAIN_ID is stored, it is never compared against block.chainid at runtime in _recoverSignerFromPayload. After a chain fork (e.g., ETH/ETC-style split), the domain separator remains valid on both chains, enabling replay of rental creation signatures. An attacker can take a valid signature from one chain and replay it on the forked chain to create unwanted rentals.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Signer constructor stores _CHAIN_ID but _recoverSignerFromPayload always uses _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR (immutable) without checking if block.chainid still matches _CHAIN_ID. This violates EIP-712's recommendation to recompute domain separator when chain ID changes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR.toTypedDataHash(payloadHash) always uses immutable separator; _CHAIN_ID stored but never checked against block.chainid

preconditions: A chain fork occurs creating two chains with different chain IDs
Protocol is deployed on both chains (or the forked chain has the same deployment)
Valid signatures exist from before the fork

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Domain separator is immutable, never recomputed at runtime
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Pre-fork signatures valid on both chains; attacker replays on forked chain
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Unauthorized rental creation on forked chain, potentially locking victim assets

economic_questions: How likely is a chain fork for target chains (Ethereum, Polygon, Avalanche)?
What is the window of valid unexpired signatures available for replay?
What economic damage can unauthorized rentals cause?
validation_requirements: Simulate fork scenario with same domain separator on both chains
Verify signature produced for chain A validates on chain B post-fork
Assess payload expiration as a mitigating factor

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of assets that can be locked in unauthorized rentals

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users with assets on the forked chain
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All protocol assets on forked chain

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if payload expiration provides sufficient mitigation
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm _CHAIN_ID is never checked at runtime

threats.title: Rental Order Type Hash Uses abi.encode Instead of abi.encodePacked for Nested Type Strings
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveRentalTypehashes function computes _RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH using abi.encode(rentalOrderTypeString, hookTypeString, itemTypeString) instead of abi.encodePacked. Per EIP-712, the struct type hash is keccak256 of the concatenated type strings without ABI padding. abi.encode adds 32-byte offset/length headers between the dynamic byte arrays, producing a completely different hash. While the protocol uses this wrong hash consistently internally, any off-chain system following the EIP-712 standard will compute a different type hash, causing all rental order hash verifications to fail.
threats.investigation_rationale: The rentPayloadTypeHash at Signer.sol correctly uses abi.encodePacked, but rentalOrderTypeHash uses abi.encode. This inconsistency within the same file confirms the abi.encode usage is a bug, not a design choice.

proof_of_signal.pattern: rentalOrderTypeHash = keccak256(abi.encode(rentalOrderTypeString, hookTypeString, itemTypeString)) — should be abi.encodePacked like rentPayloadTypeHash at line 313

preconditions: Off-chain verification or integration tools attempt standard EIP-712 computation

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: abi.encode adds ~192 bytes of ABI padding vs abi.encodePacked for 3 dynamic byte arrays
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Off-chain/on-chain hash mismatch breaks interoperability; inconsistency between rentalOrderTypeHash and rentPayloadTypeHash encoding methods in same file
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Integration failure for rental order verification; potential for hash collision under non-standard encoding

economic_questions: Does the off-chain signer use abi.encode or abi.encodePacked for this type hash?

Verify which version the off-chain signer uses
Test stopRent with externally-computed rental order hashes
